item.aggregated_output:
# GUIDELINES

1. See common [RUST.md](./DNA/languages/RUST.md) guideline
2. When develop new REST API use [API.md](./DNA/REST/API.md), [STATUS_CODES](./DNA/REST/STATUS_CODES.md)
3. When develop new Module use [ModKit Unified System](../docs/modkit_unified_system/README.md)
4. Check [SECURITY.md](./SECURITY.md) for security best practices.
5. Review [DEPENDENCIES.md](./DEPENDENCIES.md) for preferred dependencies and libraries.

arguments.summary_title: bug6 fixed: single-character OData identifiers parse correctly
arguments.summary_markdown:
The bug was in the PEG identifier rule in `libs/modkit-odata/src/odata_parse.rs`: it required an initial identifier character followed by one or more suffix characters, so a valid one-character field name such as `x` was rejected before the comparison could parse.

The fix changed the suffix portion of the identifier grammar to zero-or-more characters while preserving the existing first-character restriction. This allows `x eq 1` and still prevents identifiers from starting with a digit or other invalid character.

Coverage was added in `libs/modkit-odata/src/odata_parse_tests.rs` with `single_character_identifier`, which parses `parse_str("x eq 1")` and asserts an equality comparison between identifier `x` and numeric value `1`.

Validation passed with `cargo build -p cf-modkit-odata --lib`, the focused `cargo test -p cf-modkit-odata --lib single_character_identifier`, and the full package lib test run `cargo test -p cf-modkit-odata --lib` with 95 passing tests. There were 0 refinement cycles. The only misleading signal was a Go-style `./...` command error, which was not applicable to this Cargo workspace and did not affect validation.

For a similar parser bug, the shortest reliable path is to inspect the grammar quantifier at the reported location, add a minimal regression test for the exact accepted syntax, and validate both focused and package-level Cargo tests.

lessons.description: The parse error at column 2 mapped directly to an identifier suffix quantifier requiring at least one additional character, making the grammar rule itself the primary failure point rather than later OData comparison handling.
A focused regression test for `parse_str("x eq 1")` was enough to prove the one-character identifier path and guard the exact boundary case that multi-character identifier tests missed.
The reported `./...` command error came from an incompatible Go-style validation command; Cargo package build and test commands were the applicable evidence for this Rust workspace.
